title: Multi-photon schemes for mid-infrared detection
authors: Alistair H. Duff, J. E. Sipe
abstract: We calculate the theoretical non-degenerate two photon absorption and three color injected current response tensors for bulk GaAs and Ge$_{1-x}$Sn$_x$ for a range of alloy compositions. In particular, by including a ''pump'' beam we compare two ''schemes'' that are sensitive to mid-infrared photons. In ''scheme I'' we consider GaAs and a pump photon with energy greater than half the band gap, and in ''scheme II'' we consider Ge$_{1-x}$Sn$_x$ with a pump photon with energy less than half the band gap. We find that for certain pump and alloy concentrations Ge$_{1-x}$Sn$_x$ has a substantially larger nonlinear response and three-color injected current than GaAs in the mid-infrared frequency window where both materials can absorb photons via non-degenerate two-photon absorption.
summary: This paper investigates theoretical methods for detecting mid-infrared light using multi-photon processes in semiconductors. It compares a scheme using wide-gap GaAs to one using narrow-gap Germanium-Tin alloys. The researchers demonstrate that the GeSn-based approach provides much higher sensitivity and larger nonlinear current generation. This work is significant for developing room-temperature infrared detectors.
main_problem: Developing efficient mid-infrared (MIR) detection methods that avoid cryogenic temperatures by comparing two multi-photon absorption schemes using non-degenerate two-photon absorption (ND-2PA).
main_result: The study finds that Ge(1-x)Sn(x) alloys (Scheme II) exhibit a substantially larger nonlinear response and three-color injected current compared to GaAs (Scheme I) in the mid-infrared window.
method: Theoretical calculations using first- and second-order time-dependent perturbation theory and a 30-band k.p model.
model_or_system: The physical systems studied are bulk GaAs and Ge(1-x)Sn(x) alloys, modeled using a 30-band k.p Hamiltonian to account for the entire Brillouin zone and anisotropic nonlinear responses.
key_observables: Nonlinear absorption coefficient (alpha), three-color injected current response tensor, linear electric susceptibility, and anisotropy/dichroism parameters.
important_parameters: Photon energies (signal and pump), alloy composition (x), band gap energy, and pump intensities up to 1 TW/cm2.
main_assumptions: Independent particle approximation, continuous wave limit, neglect of many-body effects, neglect of phonon-mediated indirect absorption, and neglect of local inversion symmetry breaking in GeSn alloys.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of current generation via interference between degenerate 2PA and 1PA; Figure 2 shows schematics of the two ND-2PA schemes (Scheme I and Scheme II).
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of MIR detection, defines two multi-photon schemes, details the 30-band k.p modeling approach, compares the nonlinear responses and current injection of GaAs and GeSn, and provides technical parameters for the alloys.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: Schematic representation of the generation of current through the interference of degenerate 2PA (D-2PA) at the fundamental frequency ω and 1PA at the harmonic 2ω. The larger ovals on the right indicate constructive interference for the electron-hole transition at positive k, versus destructive interference at −k.

FIG. 2: Schematic representation of the ND-2PA process for the same signal photon but different pump and band gap selections. The bold red arrows represent the pump photon energy, and the narrow blue arrows the signal photon energy.
FIG. 3: GaAs band structure along two high symmetry directions in the BZ, calculated with the 30-band k · p model.
FIG. 4: Evolution of the direct and indirect band gap with increasing Sn concentration, as calculated with the 30-band k · p model.
FIG. 5: Ge and Ge0.83Sn0.17 band structure along two high symmetry directions in the BZ, calculated with the 30-band k · p model.
FIG. 6: Representation of where in the parameter space of signal and pump frequencies relative to the material band gap scheme I and scheme II exist.
FIG. 7: 1PA of GaAs as a function of incident photon energy ℏω near the band gap, calculated with the 30-band k · p model.

FIG. 8: ND-2PA of GaAs as a function of signal photon energy ℏωs in the presence of four different pump energies ℏωp, calculated with the 30-band k · p model. Only the largest component α(2) xxxx is shown.
FIG. 9: The three independent components of the D-2PA tensor of GaAs as a function of incident photon energy ℏω, calculated with the 30-band k · p model.

FIG. 10: The spectral dependence of the anisotropy and linear-circular dichroism for D-2PA and ND-2PA with a pump at ℏωp = 1.3 eV, calculated with the 30 band k · p GaAs model. σ plotted in black uses the left axis, while δ plotted in red uses the right axis.
